sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a male, presents with a 2-3 week history of right shoulder pain, exacerbated by movement and activity, with no restriction in arm movement but tenderness on the outside of the shoulder when raising the arm.
The pain is dull and worse with activity.
The patient denies numbness in the arm or hands.
Past medical history is significant for a previous injury to the same shoulder while playing baseball, but this current episode is described as different.
The patient has been taking Advil and icing the area with some relief.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: The patient denies numbness in the arm or hands.
Current medications: I prescribed Advil for pain management.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 97.8°F, heart rate is 62, respirations are 16, blood pressure is 116/84, and oxygen saturation is 99%.

Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion of the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus, but no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
The hand grip is good, and neurovascular exam of the right arm shows capillary refill less than 3 seconds and intact sensation to touch.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder x-ray shows no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, an injury to the tendons of the muscles that help make the shoulder muscles.
Problem list: Right shoulder pain; rotator cuff tendinopathy; possible foot and ankle pain.
Plan:
I ordered an MRI to further evaluate the shoulder and to rule out any other potential issues.
I referred the patient to physical therapy for approximately 6-8 weeks to help manage the pain and improve range of motion.
I advised the patient to continue taking Tylenol and to consider a steroid injection if symptoms do not improve.
I also suggested soaking the patient's feet in Epsom salts to help relax the muscles and reduce soreness.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with the physical therapist for the 6-8 week treatment plan.
